content:
5 min read
Just now

--

The lesson from social media
Most major technologies arrive wrapped in social good. The consequences emerge later, after the behaviour has already scaled.
Social media was not introduced to most people as a machine for polarisation, anxiety, outrage, surveillance advertising or democratic disruption. It arrived as connection.
It promised:
• connection across distance
• community
• voice for the unheard
• discovery
• creativity
• civic participation
• small business reach
• access to information
• belonging
Much of that was real. That is why people adopted it. The mistake was not believing it had benefits. The mistake was failing to see that a technology can be useful at the individual level and destructive at the system level once scaled through a business model.
That is the lesson for AI.
The promise of AI
We have seen this pattern before. A tool that begins as empowerment can reshape behaviour, incentives and social norms before society has language for the cost.
For AI, the early promise is:
• productivity
• creativity
• access to expertise
• personalised learning
• emotional support
• better writing
• faster decisions
• lower friction
• help for people who feel isolated or overwhelmed
Again, much of that will be real.
The risk is what happens when those benefits scale into dependency, private confirmation and weakened social testing.
Loss of social friction
Human beings are shaped by other people pushing back. We learn through delay, disagreement, awkwardness, repair, apology, embarrassment and consequence.
AI can reduce that friction. That is useful when we need first-draft thinking. It becomes dangerous if we stop taking our thoughts into real relationships where they can be tested.
People may become more articulate without becoming more accountable.
They can produce a polished version of themselves before they have done the harder work of reflection, repair or change.
Private realities become easier to build
Social media created group realities. AI may create private ones.
With social media, people could find others who shared their worldview. With AI, a person may not need a group. They can have a private system that organises their view, reassures them, gives language to their grievance and helps them act from it.
Reality may become more personalised and less socially tested.
That is deeper than echo chambers. It is not only “people like me agree.” It is “the machine that knows me says this makes sense.”
Emotional outsourcing
People already use AI to decide what to say, how to feel, whether they are overreacting, how to interpret someone’s behaviour and what action to take.
Some of that is helpful. But over time, the habit may become:
I do not know what I think until I ask the machine.
AI may become an emotional regulator, not just an information tool.
That could weaken people’s tolerance for uncertainty, discomfort and unresolved emotion.
Confidence without contact
AI can make a view sound coherent very quickly. It can turn a messy feeling into a structured explanation.
That feels productive. But some thoughts need contact with other people before they become decisions. Some interpretations need reality-testing. Some feelings need time.
People may act from narratives that sound complete before they have been tested.
That matters in relationships, workplaces, politics, health, money and identity.
Authority without responsibility
A human who confirms us has some social stake. They know us. They may have to live with the consequences. They may be challenged later.
AI can sound authoritative without carrying responsibility in the same human way.
The user may experience guidance, but responsibility remains blurred.
When the advice is useful, AI feels like a partner. When it causes harm, the accountability becomes harder to locate.
Weaker shared judgement
Shared reality depends on people bringing their perceptions into contact with others. If more of that process moves into private AI interactions, fewer beliefs may be tested in shared spaces.
Judgement becomes more private, more polished and less exposed to correction.
That is a serious societal shift.
What people feel justified doing
AI can change what people feel justified doing.
Once a subjective experience feels validated, people may act.
They may send the message.
Make the complaint.
End the relationship.
Refuse the evidence.
Confront the person.
Join the group.
Share the claim.
Escalate the situation.
Certainty without evidence
AI can strengthen certainty without increasing evidence.
In shared reality, social verification can make a subjective experience feel objective. With AI, a similar effect can happen without new evidence being introduced. A person may begin with a tentative interpretation: “I think this is what happened.” The system can then return a fluent explanation of why that interpretation makes sense.
The person may leave the exchange feeling more certain, but the underlying evidence may not have improved. What has changed is the coherence of the explanation. The view has been organised, expanded and made easier to defend.
That is a serious risk because confidence can grow faster than accuracy.
The privatisation of shared reality
This also changes where reality-testing happens.
Social media fragmented shared reality into groups. People could find others who shared their worldview and build a sense of common reality around that group. AI may go further by creating personalised, private confirmation loops.
With social media, the feeling was often: “People like me see this too.”
With AI, the feeling can become: “The system that understands me says this makes sense.”
That is a different kind of confirmation. It is quieter, more intimate and less visible to others. Reality-testing may move from public or social spaces into private machine interactions, which makes correction harder because no one else may see the process by which the belief became stronger.
The societal consequence
The consequences would not be confined to individual users.
Public debate could become harder to repair because people would arrive with positions that have already been organised, rehearsed and justified in private. Corrections could feel less persuasive because the original belief has already been made coherent. Disagreement could become more defensive because challenge is no longer meeting a loose opinion. It is meeting something that already feels considered.
This would make trust harder to rebuild. Institutions no longer compete only with misinformation or public opinion; they compete with personalised private reasoning that feels fluent, emotionally satisfying and already complete. That is difficult to counter with the slower work of public reasoning.
What kind of public life remains?
AI does not need to destroy shared reality to weaken society as we know it. It only needs to make private certainty easier than shared reasoning.
That is the deeper risk. A society does not fall only when people believe false things. It begins to weaken when people lose the habit, patience and trust required to test reality together.
If more of our fears, grievances and interpretations are organised in private by systems that sound as though they understand us, then public reasoning has to work harder to reach us. Evidence has to compete with coherence. Correction has to compete with reassurance. Institutions have to compete with explanations that feel personally made.
The fall of society as we know it would not necessarily arrive as collapse. It may arrive more quietly, through a thousand small withdrawals from shared reality: fewer people willing to be corrected, fewer people able to sit with uncertainty, fewer people prepared to trust any process that does not already confirm what they feel.
That is why the issue is not only what AI can produce. It is what kind of public life remains possible when private confirmation becomes easier, faster and more emotionally satisfying than the shared work of finding out what is true.
